content:
Certainly! The question "Do you only go out to bars on weekends?" is asking whether going to bars is something you do exclusively on weekends, implying that weekends are the only time you visit bars.

When someone answers, "No, I go to bars every day," they are saying that the statement in the question is not true. Specifically:

- The question suggests that the person goes to bars only on weekends.
- The answer "No" denies that restriction.
- By adding "I go to bars every day," the person clarifies that they visit bars not just on weekends but also on weekdays.

This is a common pattern in English where a question with "only" implies exclusivity, and the negative answer denies that exclusivity.

In short, the question assumes a limitation ("only on weekends"), and the answer rejects that limitation by stating a broader or different reality ("every day"). This is why the answer to "Do you only go out to bars on weekends?" can be "No, I go to bars every day." It means the person goes out to bars on more days than just weekends.
